abstract: Import and export a certificate from a file.
overview:
## Overview

Certificates are not secret and you often want to share them to disseminate a public key, but [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecCertificate] is an opaque type that you can’t distribute directly. Instead, you create a Distinguished Encoding Rules (DER) encoded data representation of the certificate using the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecCertificateCopyData(_:)] function:

You might send this data object over a network connection or store it in a `.cer` file:

When you receive such a data object, you use the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecCertificateCreateWithData(_:_:)] function to reverse the process:

By leaving the first argument empty, you rely on the default allocator to allocate memory for the certificate. Note that in Objective-C, you call [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/CoreFoundation/CFRelease] to free the certificate’s memory when you are done with it. In Swift, the system manages the object’s memory automatically.

# Storing a DER-Encoded X.509 Certificate

**Article**

Import and export a certificate from a file.

## Overview

Certificates are not secret and you often want to share them to disseminate a public key, but [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecCertificate] is an opaque type that you can’t distribute directly. Instead, you create a Distinguished Encoding Rules (DER) encoded data representation of the certificate using the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecCertificateCopyData(_:)] function:



You might send this data object over a network connection or store it in a `.cer` file:



When you receive such a data object, you use the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecCertificateCreateWithData(_:_:)] function to reverse the process:



By leaving the first argument empty, you rely on the default allocator to allocate memory for the certificate. Note that in Objective-C, you call [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/CoreFoundation/CFRelease] to free the certificate’s memory when you are done with it. In Swift, the system manages the object’s memory automatically.
title: Storing a DER-Encoded X.509 Certificate
